section.title: The Temple Built and Furnished
section.sectionRationale: With materials and craftsmen ready, Solomon finally builds. The Chronicler describes the temple's dimensions, its overlay of gold, the towering cherubim, the veil, and the two great pillars at the entrance, then moves outward to the bronze altar, the molten Sea, the ten basins, ten lampstands, and ten tables, until every article of gold and bronze for worship stands finished and ready.
section.thesis: Every measurement, every ounce of gold, and every bronze basin in these two chapters testifies that the temple was built with lavish, unhurried care as a fitting house for the Name of the LORD, and its two pillars, named He establishes and In Him is strength, quietly proclaim the very confidence the whole book will keep returning to.
section.frameNote: Five units: the site and foundation, 3:1-7; the Most Holy Place and the cherubim, 3:8-14; the pillars Jachin and Boaz, 3:15-17; the bronze altar and molten Sea, 4:1-5; the basins, lampstands, and tables, 4:6-10; and the completion of every bronze and gold furnishing, 4:11-22.

units.label: The site and foundation
units.note: on Mount Moriah, the threshing floor of Ornan

verses.text: Then Solomon began to build the house of the LORD in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah, where the LORD had appeared to his father David. This was the place that David had prepared on the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.
Solomon began construction on the second day of the second month in the fourth year of his reign.
The foundation that Solomon laid for the house of God was sixty cubits long and twenty cubits wide, according to the old standard.
The portico at the front, extending across the width of the temple, was twenty cubits long and twenty cubits high. He overlaid the inside with pure gold.
He paneled the main room with cypress, which he overlaid with fine gold and decorated with palm trees and chains.
He adorned the temple with precious stones for beauty, and its gold was from Parvaim.
He overlaid its beams, thresholds, walls, and doors with gold, and he carved cherubim on the walls.

units.label: The Most Holy Place and the cherubim

verses.text: Then he made the {{qodeshHaqodashim|Most Holy Place}}, its length corresponded to the width of the temple, twenty cubits long and twenty cubits wide. And he overlaid the inside with six hundred talents of fine gold.
The weight of the nails was fifty shekels of gold. He also overlaid the upper rooms with gold.
In the Most Holy Place he made two {{keruvim|cherubim}} of sculptured work, and he overlaid them with gold.

The total wingspan of the cherubim was twenty cubits. One wing of the first cherub was five cubits long and touched the wall of the temple, and its other wing was five cubits long and touched the wing of the other cherub.
The wing of the second cherub also measured five cubits and touched the wall of the temple, while its other wing measured five cubits and touched the wing of the first cherub.
So the total wingspan of these cherubim was twenty cubits. They stood on their feet, facing the main room.
He made the veil of blue, purple, and crimson yarn and fine linen, with cherubim woven into it.

units.label: The pillars Jachin and Boaz
units.note: He establishes, in Him is strength

verses.text: In front of the temple he made two pillars, which together were thirty-five cubits high, each with a capital on top measuring five cubits.
He made interwoven chains and put them on top of the pillars. He made a hundred pomegranates and fastened them into the chainwork.
Then he set up the pillars in front of the temple, one on the south and one on the north. The pillar on the south he named {{yakin|Jachin}}, and the pillar on the north he named {{boazPillar|Boaz}}.

units.label: The bronze altar and the molten Sea

verses.text: He made a bronze altar twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and ten cubits high.
He also made the Sea of cast metal. It was circular in shape, measuring ten cubits from rim to rim, five cubits in height, and thirty cubits in circumference.
Below the rim, figures of oxen encircled it, ten per cubit all the way around the Sea, cast in two rows as a part of the Sea.
The Sea stood on twelve oxen, three facing north, three facing west, three facing south, and three facing east. The Sea rested on them, with all their hindquarters toward the center.
It was a handbreadth thick, and its rim was fashioned like the brim of a cup, like a lily blossom. It could hold three thousand baths.

units.label: The basins, lampstands, and tables
units.note: five on the south, five on the north

verses.text: He also made ten basins for washing and placed five on the south side and five on the north. The parts of the burnt offering were rinsed in them, but the priests used the Sea for washing.
He made ten gold lampstands according to their specifications and placed them in the temple, five on the south side and five on the north.
Additionally, he made ten tables and placed them in the temple, five on the south side and five on the north. He also made a hundred gold bowls.
He made the courtyard of the priests and the large court with its doors, and he overlaid the doors with bronze.
He put the Sea on the south side, at the southeast corner.

units.label: Completion of every bronze and gold furnishing
units.note: the weight of the bronze could not be determined

verses.text: Additionally, Huram made the pots, shovels, and sprinkling bowls. So Huram finished the work that he had undertaken for King Solomon in the house of God:
the two pillars, the two bowl-shaped capitals atop the pillars, the two sets of network covering both bowls of the capitals atop the pillars,
the four hundred pomegranates for the two sets of network, two rows of pomegranates for each network covering both the bowl-shaped capitals atop the pillars,
the stands, the basins on the stands,
the Sea, the twelve oxen underneath the Sea,
and the pots, shovels, meat forks, and all the other articles. All these objects that Huram-abi made for King Solomon for the house of the LORD were of polished bronze.
The king had them cast in clay molds in the plain of the Jordan between Succoth and Zeredah.
Solomon made all these articles in such great abundance that the weight of the bronze could not be determined.
Solomon also made all the furnishings for the house of God: the golden altar, the tables on which was placed the Bread of the Presence,
the lampstands of pure gold and their lamps, to burn in front of the inner sanctuary as prescribed,
the flowers, lamps, and tongs of gold, of purest gold,
the wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, ladles, and censers of pure gold, and the gold doors of the temple: the inner doors to the Most Holy Place as well as the doors of the main hall.

qodeshHaqodashim.gloss: The Most Holy Place, or Holy of Holies, the innermost chamber where the ark would rest beneath the cherubim's wings. Its perfect cube shaped proportions and the overlay of six hundred talents of gold mark it as the most sacred space on earth under the old covenant, the place where God's presence dwelt most intensely among His people.

keruvim.gloss: Cherubim, the winged heavenly beings whose great sculpted forms overshadow the Most Holy Place with a combined wingspan of twenty cubits. Cherubim guarded the way to the tree of life after the fall, Genesis 3:24, so their presence here signals that the temple is a place where the way back to God, barred since Eden, is being reopened under careful, holy conditions.

yakin.gloss: Jachin, the name of the pillar set on the south side of the temple entrance, probably meaning He establishes. Standing at the very threshold of God's house, its name is itself a silent sermon: it is the LORD who establishes the throne, the temple, and the whole covenant people, not their own strength.

boazPillar.gloss: Boaz, the name of the pillar set on the north side of the temple entrance, probably meaning in Him is strength. Paired with Jachin, He establishes, the two pillars together proclaim at the very doorway of worship that all stability and strength belong to the LORD alone, not to the temple's gold or the size of Solomon's kingdom.

basic.context: These two chapters describe the actual construction of the temple, from its foundation on Mount Moriah, the very site of David's prepared threshing floor, through the gold overlaid Most Holy Place with its towering cherubim, the great bronze Sea resting on twelve oxen, and every lamp, table, and basin needed for worship. The sheer quantity of gold and bronze, described almost breathlessly, insists that nothing was too costly for this house.
basic.hermeneutics: This is technical, historical description of a specific building, not a pattern believers are meant to replicate in church architecture today. What is theologically significant is what the details communicate: extravagant care befitting the presence of God, cherubim recalling the guarded way back to Eden, and pillars whose very names confess that establishment and strength belong to the LORD, not to human achievement.
basic.originalLanguages: At the heart of the complex stands the qodesh haqodashim, the Most Holy Place, overshadowed by keruvim, cherubim, whose wings recall the guardians once set at Eden's gate. At the entrance stand two pillars named yakin, He establishes, and boaz, in Him is strength, so that everyone entering to worship passes literally between two proclamations that all stability comes from the LORD.
basic.typology: The cherubim guarding the Most Holy Place recall the cherubim who guarded Eden after the fall, and both point forward to Christ, who tears the veil and opens permanent access into God's presence, Hebrews 10:19-20. The pillars Jachin and Boaz, He establishes and in Him is strength, anticipate Christ, the true foundation and cornerstone on whom the whole living temple, the church, is established and finds its strength, 1 Corinthians 3:11, Ephesians 2:20-21.
basic.christ: The Most Holy Place, so costly and so hedged about with cherubim, points to the greater sanctuary Christ entered once for all by His own blood, Hebrews 9:11-12, tearing the veil that once separated worshipers from God's presence. The pillars' names, He establishes and in Him is strength, describe exactly what Christ is for His people, the sure foundation, Isaiah 28:16, and the strength in whom they stand, Ephesians 6:10.
basic.god: God is worthy of the very best His people can offer: gold beyond counting, skilled craftsmanship, and painstaking care in every measurement. Yet the temple's own architecture confesses that human strength and stability come from Him alone, not from gold or grandeur, and that the way into His presence, guarded since Eden, is being carefully, graciously reopened.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 6 to 7 give the parallel and more detailed account of the temple's construction. Genesis 3:24 first introduces the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life. Hebrews 9:11-12 describes Christ entering the greater, heavenly sanctuary. And 1 Corinthians 3:11 names Christ as the only foundation that can ever be laid.
basic.oneStory: Every gold overlaid wall and bronze basin in these chapters testifies to lavish devotion to God, while the cherubim recall Eden's guarded gate and the pillars' names quietly confess that establishment and strength belong to the LORD alone. All of it points forward to Christ, the true and greater sanctuary, the sure foundation on whom His people, the living temple, are established and stand strong.

context.literary: The Chronicler moves the reader inward and then outward: first the temple structure itself and its innermost sanctuary, 3:1-14, then the pillars marking its public threshold, 3:15-17, then outward into the courtyard furnishings, the altar, Sea, basins, lampstands, and tables, 4:1-10, closing with a summary inventory of every bronze and gold article completed, 4:11-22, giving the whole section the feel of an inventory of devotion, item by item, ounce by ounce.
context.historical: Mount Moriah, identified here explicitly with David's threshing floor purchase from Ornan the Jebusite, 1 Chronicles 21, ties the temple site to both the near sacrifice of Isaac, Genesis 22:2, and David's own act of costly worship after the plague. The scale of gold, six hundred talents for the inner sanctuary alone, and the vast bronze Sea, reflect the wealth and craftsmanship of the united monarchy at its height, requiring imported skill from Tyre and specialized casting in the Jordan valley's clay.
context.canonical: This unit parallels 1 Kings 6 to 7 but the Chronicler compresses architectural detail to highlight theological resonances his readers, without a temple of their own, most needed: the cherubim recalling the guarded way to God, and the pillar names proclaiming that establishment and strength are gifts of the LORD, truths that outlast any building, even one as fallen as their former temple.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record with careful, loving detail the construction of the temple as an act of extravagant devotion to God, and, through the names of its pillars and the presence of the cherubim, to teach that human strength and stability rest not in gold or grandeur but in the LORD who establishes and strengthens His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapters describe a specific, unrepeatable building project with exact ancient measurements and materials; they do not prescribe architectural requirements for churches today. What is enduring is the theology embedded in the details: the seriousness owed to approaching God's presence, and the truth that all establishment and strength belong to Him.

debates.question: What is the significance of naming the two entrance pillars Jachin and Boaz?

views.label: A proclamation over the dynasty and temple
views.case: Meaning He establishes and in Him is strength, the names may function almost like a royal inscription, declaring over every worshiper entering that it is the LORD who establishes David's throne and the temple's permanence, not human strength, echoing the covenant promise of 2 Samuel 7.

views.label: A liturgical confession spoken at worship
views.case: Some suggest the names formed part of temple liturgy, perhaps recited or sung as worshipers passed between the pillars, turning architecture itself into a confession of faith recited at every approach to God.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the pillars were never merely decorative; whether royal inscription or liturgical confession, they proclaimed in stone what the whole book insists on: strength and stability belong to the LORD alone.

study.originalLanguages: At the center of the whole structure stands the qodesh haqodashim, the Most Holy Place, overshadowed by keruvim, cherubim, whose towering wings recall the guardians once stationed at Eden's gate, Genesis 3:24, signaling that this house is where the way back to God's presence is being carefully reopened. At its public threshold stand two pillars, yakin, He establishes, and boaz, in Him is strength, so that every worshiper passing into the courts walks quite literally between a confession that all stability and might belong to the LORD.

typology.type: The cherubim overshadowing the Most Holy Place
typology.fulfillment: Christ, through whom the way into God's presence, guarded since Eden, is opened once for all, Hebrews 10:19-20.

typology.type: The pillars Jachin and Boaz, He establishes and in Him is strength
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the sure foundation and cornerstone on whom His people are established, and the strength in whom they stand, Isaiah 28:16, Ephesians 2:20-21, 6:10.

typology.type: The bronze Sea for the priests' washing
typology.fulfillment: The cleansing Christ provides for His people to draw near to God, Hebrews 10:22.

study.christ: The Most Holy Place, hedged about by towering cherubim and overlaid with staggering quantities of gold, points forward to the greater and more perfect sanctuary that Christ entered once for all, not with the blood of goats and calves but with His own blood, securing an eternal redemption, Hebrews 9:11-12, and tearing the veil so that all who trust Him may draw near. The pillars' very names, Jachin, He establishes, and Boaz, in Him is strength, describe precisely what Christ is for His church: the sure foundation laid in Zion, Isaiah 28:16, 1 Peter 2:6, and the strength in which His people stand firm, Ephesians 6:10.
study.god: God is worthy of the costliest devotion His people can bring, reflected in the gold, bronze, and craftsmanship poured into this house without reservation. Yet the temple's own design confesses that human stability and strength never come from wealth or grandeur but from the LORD Himself, who establishes His people and is their strength, and who, through the cherubim recalling Eden, is graciously reopening the way into His presence rather than merely guarding it shut.

crossReferences.note: The fuller parallel account of the temple's construction and furnishing.
Cherubim guard the way to the tree of life after the fall, the same beings sculpted over the Most Holy Place.
Christ enters the greater, heavenly sanctuary once for all by His own blood.
A cornerstone chosen and precious is laid in Zion, echoing the sure foundation the pillar Jachin proclaims.

study.oneStory: Every measurement of gold and bronze in these chapters declares that nothing is too costly for the house of the LORD, while the cherubim recall the guarded way back to Eden and the pillars' names confess that all establishment and strength belong to God alone. This whole structure, however magnificent, only ever pointed forward to Christ, the true and greater sanctuary who tears the veil, and the sure foundation on whom His people, the living temple, are established and stand strong forever.

quiz.q: Where was the temple built, and why was that site significant?

options.text: On Mount Moriah, at the threshing floor David had prepared, the same place where the LORD had appeared to David.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly names the site and its significance, tying it to David's own encounter with the LORD.

options.text: On a random hill outside Jerusalem with no prior significance.
options.note: The site is specifically Mount Moriah and David's prepared threshing floor, not an unremarkable location.

options.text: At Gibeon, where the old tabernacle stood.
options.note: Gibeon was the site of the earlier tabernacle worship in chapter 1; the temple itself is built in Jerusalem on Mount Moriah.

options.text: The text gives no location at all.
options.note: Verse 1 clearly names Mount Moriah and Ornan's threshing floor.

quiz.q: What overshadowed the ark's place in the Most Holy Place?

options.text: Two large gold overlaid cherubim with a combined wingspan of twenty cubits.
options.note: Verses 10-13 describe the cherubim and their wingspan in detail.

options.text: A plain curtain with no images at all.
options.note: While there was a veil, verse 14, it was woven with cherubim, and the inner sanctuary itself held the two large sculpted cherubim.

options.text: A bronze altar.
options.note: The bronze altar stood in the courtyard, not inside the Most Holy Place, which held the cherubim.

options.text: Nothing; the Most Holy Place was left completely empty.
options.note: The chapter describes the two large cherubim placed there, verses 10-13.

quiz.q: What were the two pillars at the temple's entrance named, and what do the names likely mean?

options.text: Jachin and Boaz, probably meaning He establishes and in Him is strength.
options.note: Verse 17 names both pillars, and the footnotes on their probable meanings match this.

options.text: Alpha and Omega.
options.note: These titles are used of Christ in Revelation, not the temple pillars' names in Chronicles.

options.text: Faith and Hope.
options.note: The pillars are named Jachin and Boaz, not abstract virtues.

options.text: The pillars had no names at all.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly records both names.

quiz.q: What was the great bronze Sea used for, and what supported it?

options.text: It was a large basin of cast metal used for washing, supported on the backs of twelve bronze oxen.
options.note: 4:2-6 describes the Sea's dimensions, its use, and the twelve oxen beneath it.

options.text: It was a decorative pool with no practical use.
options.note: The Sea served a practical purpose in priestly washing, alongside the ten basins, verse 6.

options.text: It held the ark of the covenant.
options.note: The ark rested in the Most Holy Place beneath the cherubim, not in the bronze Sea.

options.text: It was made of wood.
options.note: The Sea was cast of bronze, or molten metal, as described in 4:2.

quiz.q: How do the cherubim in the temple connect to the rest of Scripture?

options.text: They recall the cherubim who guarded the way to the tree of life after the fall, and anticipate Christ opening permanent access to God's presence.
options.note: Genesis 3:24 and Hebrews 10:19-20 trace this connection from Eden's guarded gate to Christ's opened way.

options.text: They are unique to the temple and appear nowhere else in Scripture.
options.note: Cherubim appear as early as Genesis 3:24 at Eden's gate, so the connection long predates the temple.

options.text: They represent purely decorative artwork with no theological meaning.
options.note: Their placement over the Most Holy Place and their echo of Eden carries clear theological weight, not mere decoration.

options.text: They symbolize Israel's enemies being defeated.
options.note: Cherubim in Scripture function as guardians of God's presence, not symbols of defeated enemies.

questions: The temple was built with staggering, costly care. What would it look like for you to bring that same wholehearted devotion to your worship and service this week?

The cherubim guarding Eden and overshadowing the Most Holy Place remind us that access to God was once barred and carefully guarded. How does knowing Christ has opened that way change how you approach God in prayer?
The pillars Jachin and Boaz proclaimed that establishment and strength belong to the LORD, not to human achievement. Where are you tempted to rely on your own strength rather than resting in His?
Every measurement in these chapters reflects intentional, unhurried craftsmanship. Where in your own life could you slow down and give more careful attention to something you do for God?
Christ is described as the sure foundation and the strength in whom His people stand. What situation this week calls you to lean on His strength rather than your own?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote explains that the old standard cubit was about 18 inches, distinct from the longer cubit of about 21 inches used later in Ezekiel's temple vision.
Some Septuagint and Syriac manuscripts differ from the Masoretic Text's reading of 120 cubits high for the portico; the BSB footnote records this textual variant.
The BSB footnotes give the probable meanings of the pillar names: Jachin likely means He establishes, and Boaz likely means in Him is strength.

The BSB footnote records a numerical variant with 1 Kings 7:26, which gives the Sea's capacity as two thousand baths rather than three thousand; the difference may reflect measurement at different fill levels.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Chronicles 3:1-4:22. No em dashes or en dashes remain; all such marks in the source were converted to commas.
